titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Ming Fay Papers MSS.599
titlestmt.author: Rachel Searcy and Aki Snyder

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-07-01 18:18:29 UTC.

did.unittitle: Ming Fay Papers

physdesc.extent: in 24 manuscript boxes, 2 flat boxes, 4 oversized flat boxes, 16 flat file folders, 5 folders in a shared manuscript box, and 4 folders in a shared oversized flat box

did.abstract: Ming Fay (Chinese: 费明杰, 1943–2025) was a sculptor and art professor born in Shanghai and based in New York City. Fay was widely recognized for his sculptures and large-scale public art installations. His work used the garden as a metaphor for utopia and explored the relationship between humans and nature. In addition to his artistic practice, Fay taught at the Pratt Institute in New York City and William Paterson University in Wayne, New Jersey. The Ming Fay Papers (dated 1965–2014) comprise materials Fay created and collected over the course of his career as both an artist and educator. The collection includes proposals and project files related to both completed and unrealized public artworks; research and subject files; correspondence and administrative paperwork; exhibition announcements, catalogs, posters, and ephemera; publications; sketches on various types of paper; photographs, slides, and negatives; and press clippings.
did.physloc: Materials are stored offsite and advance notice is required for use. Please request materials at least two business days prior to your research visit to coordinate access.
did.langmaterial: The majority of materials are in English, with some materials in Chinese.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Ming Fay Papers (dated 1965–2014) comprise materials Fay created and collected over the course of his career as both an artist and educator. The collection includes proposals and project files related to both completed and unrealized public artworks; research and subject files; correspondence and administrative paperwork; exhibition announcements, catalogs, and ephemera; publications; sketches on various types of paper; photographs, slides, and negatives; and press clippings.

Series I: Artistic Works documents Ming Fay's conceptual artistic practice, including major public art commissions such as Delancey Orchard and Shad Crossing and Spiral Ears. The series further contains documentation of Fay's works, including original artworks; sketches on various types of paper; conceptual drawings, architectural plans, and project proposals; and exhibition announcements, posters, ephemera, press coverage, photographs, and related promotional materials. Examples of Fay's fruit, nut, and hanging garden sculptures exhibited in solo and group shows are documented extensively in this series.

Series II: Teaching, Lectures, and Conferences centers on Fay's academic career at both William Paterson University and the Pratt Institute, as well as lectures and conferences he attended or presented at. Materials include syllabi, project prompts, reading assignments, lecture notes, memoranda, newsletters, conference programs, and lecture flyers.

Series III: Publications and Writings contains Fay's essays, reviews, manuscripts, published works, notebooks, and sketchbooks; alongside English- and Chinese-language press coverage, interviews, and book excerpts that contextualize his artistic practice and career.

Series IV: Administrative Papers documents the administrative and professional aspects of Fay's work through correspondence; grant and residency records; resumes, biographies and artist statements; address books; loan agreements; budgets; and records related to the destruction of several of his sculptures in a fire at Hunt's Point Sculpture Park in the Bronx.

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Ming Fay (Chinese: 费明杰, 1943–2025) was a sculptor and art professor born in Shanghai and based in New York City. Fay moved to the United States in 1961 to study at Columbus College of Art and Design; received a BFA from the Kansas City Art Institute; and an MFA from the University of California, Santa Barbara. Fay was widely recognized for his sculptures and large-scale public art installations. His work used the garden as a metaphor for utopia and explored the relationship between humans and nature.

Fay exhibited his works extensively throughout the United States and internationally, and completed numerous public art commissions around the world. Fay's sculptures and installations range from large botanical forms to theatrical garden environments to immersive science fiction–inspired landscapes. His later work focused on the parallels between the symbolic meanings of plants and human desires; and investigated how the natural world reflects aspirations, anxieties, and states of being. Central to his practice was the concept of the garden as a metaphor for abundance, utopia, and humanity's enduring search for an ideal existence.

In addition to his artistic practice, Fay taught at the Pratt Institute in New York City and William Paterson University in Wayne, New Jersey. He was also a visiting professor at the Rinehart School of Sculpture at the Maryland Institute College of Art and the Chinese University of Hong Kong.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: This collection is protected by copyright and/or related rights. You are free to use materials in the collection in any way that is permitted by the copyright and related rights legislation that applies to your use. For other uses you need to obtain permission from the rights-holder(s).

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Donated by Parker Fay on behalf of Ming Fay in March 2020. The accession number associated with this gift is 2020.024.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection has been arranged into four series, one of which is arranged into three sub-series.
Subseries I.C: Non Sculpture Artwork and Source Materials
Series II: Teaching, Lectures, and Conferences
Materials are arranged in alphabetical order by topic within each series and sub-series.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Identification of item, date; Ming Fay Papers; MSS 599; box number; folder number or item identifier; Fales Library and Special Collections, New York University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: At the time of accessioning, materials were rehoused into smaller archival boxes and folders to prevent heavy boxes. A cross-walk of the original box numbers provided by Parker Fay and the current box numbers in ArchivesSpace is linked to the accession record as an external document.

In Summer 2026, this collection was arranged and described by an archivist. Since most of the materials were already housed in archival-quality folders and boxes, they remained in their current folders and boxes. Original folder titles were retained whenever possible, but new titles were assigned as needed. Oversized materials were rehoused into appropriately sized folders and boxes. Materials stored in binders or plastic sleeves were removed from those enclosures, rehoused into archival folders, and any relevant metadata was retained. After retaining two archival copies, duplicate materials were discarded in accordance with the donor agreement. Loose photographs and negatives were rehoused into archival photo sleeves; and slides were placed in stable archival enclosures. Any associated metadata was retained.
A selection of collaged art proposals and a rolled poster was stabilized and rehoused by New York University's Barbara Goldsmith Preservation and Conservation Department.

New York University Libraries follows professional standards and best practices when imaging, ingesting, and processing born-digital material in order to maintain the integrity of the content. During processing, one optical disc was forensically imaged, analyzed, and arranged using Forensics Toolkit. The electronic records were intellectually incorporated into the finding aid.

appraisal.head: Appraisal
appraisal.p: During the course of processing in Summer 2026, the following materials were removed from the collection: duplicate materials; tax documents; plant seeds; and English language publications that did not appear to mention Fay, have any personalizations from him, or personalizations for him. These materials were discarded per the donor agreement.

phystech.head: Born-Digital Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Advance notice is required for the use of computer records. Original physical digital media is restricted. An access terminal for born-digital materials in the collection is available by appointment for reading room viewing and listening only. Researchers may view an item's original container and/or carrier, but the physical carriers themselves are not available for use because of preservation concerns.

controlaccess.subject: Sculptors
Artists -- United States.
Public art -- United States.
Public sculpture, American.
Exhibitions.
controlaccess.corpname: William Paterson University of New Jersey

did.unittitle: Series I: Artistic Works

physdesc.extent: in 15 manuscript boxes, 2 flat boxes, 5 oversized flat boxes, and 16 flat file folders

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series documents Ming Fay's conceptual artistic practice through materials related to his major public art commissions, exhibitions, sources of inspiration, and creative development. It includes records of large-scale projects such as Delancey Orchard and Shad Crossing, Whitehall Crossing, Spiral Ears, Leaf Gate and Goldenrain Pier. Materials related to those public artworks include architectural plans, proposals, budgets, research files, photographs, and press coverage.

The series also highlights Fay's extensive exhibition history in the United States and abroad. Exhibitions features in this series include, but are not limited to: Nature Reborn: From Archaeology to Science Fiction, Images from Beyond, and Qian & Bao. His exhibitions are documented through photographs, reviews, announcements, posters, promotional materials, and exhibition ephemera. This series also includes examples of sketches, drawings, paintings, and calligraphy done of various types of paper that represent his artistic style. Additionally, the series contains source materials that influenced Fay's works and materials related to Fay's collaborations on performance set designs.

did.unittitle: Subseries I.A: Public Art

physdesc.extent: in 5 manuscript boxes, 4 oversized flat boxes, and 5 flat file folders

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains a wide range of materials that document Ming Fay's conceptual artistic practice. Major public arts commissions prominently documented in this series include Delancey Orchard and Shad Crossing, located in the Delancey/Essex Subway Station (commissioned by New York City's Metropolitan Transit Authority); Whitehall Crossing, located in the Whitehall Ferry Terminal of the Staten Island Ferry (commissioned by NYC's Cultural Affairs Department); Spiral Ears, located in the Philadelphia Criminal Justice Center (commissioned by Percent for Art); Leaf Gate, Keys Flight, Seed of Elm, The Spirit of the Elm, Elm in Bloom, Sprouting Buds, located at P.S. 7 in Elmhurst, Queens (commissioned by the NYC Department of Cultural Affairs); and Goldenrain Pier, in Flushing Queens (commissioned by the NYC Economic Development Corporation). Materials related to these public art commissions include architectural blueprints; project proposals, including draft drawings, budgets, and research materials; photographs; and press about the public artworks.

Subseries I.B: Exhibitions

physdesc.extent: in 11 manuscript boxes, 1 flat box, 2 oversized flat boxes, and 3 flat file folders

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries focuses on Fay's many exhibitions both in the United States and internationally. Examples of exhibitions that are prominently documented in this sub-series include: Nature Reborn: Archaeology to Science Fiction, Images from Beyond, Qian & Bao, Ming Fay: Recent Works, ORIENTations: Works of Eight Chinese-American Artists, Fabricated Nature, A Feast for the Eyes, and Floating Reeds. These exhibition materials document examples of Fay's fruit, nut, and hanging garden sculptures that have appeared in numerous solo and group shows. Materials in this sub-series include press coverage in the form of clippings, reviews, and press releases; photographs of exhibitions, studio spaces, and individual works; and exhibition announcements, promotional materials, and posters.

Subseries I.C: Non Sculpture Artwork and Source Materials

physdesc.extent: in 2 manuscript boxes, 1 flat box, 3 oversized flat boxes, and 8 flat file folders

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains materials that inspired Fay's work. These art source materials and subject files include photographs of other mosaic artworks that inspired Delancey Orchard and Shad Crossing, images of various leaf types that influenced Leaf Gate; documents on tai chi that informed his nature sculptures — some of which evolved into human-like figures performing slow tai chi–like movements; and other clippings that influenced his works. This sub-series also includes many examples of mostly untitled sketches, drawings, paintings, and calligraphy done on various types of paper -- all of which reflect Fay's creative process. Finally, Fay lent his creative expertise to some performance groups, helping to design sets. Materials related to those performances are also found in this sub-series.

Series II: Teaching, Lectures, and Conferences

physdesc.extent: in 2 manuscript boxes, 1 flat box, and 1 oversized flat box

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series primarily documents Fay's tenure as a Professor of Art at William Paterson University, while also containing materials related to his appointment at the Pratt Institute. It additionally documents lectures and conferences that Fay attended and, in some instances, participated in as a presenter. Materials in this series consist of course syllabi, project assignments, reading lists, lecture notes, conference materials, and lecture flyers. This series also includes official communications, memoranda, and newsletters issued by William Paterson University.

Series III: Publications and Writings

physdesc.extent: in 6 manuscript boxes, 1 flat box, and 2 oversized flat boxes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series focuses on articles, reviews, essays, and book excerpts written by Ming Fay, as well as those written about him. Materials include essays, books, and reviews written by Fay; a draft of his manuscript Introduction to Space and Form; and copies of publications featuring articles, essays, interviews, and book excerpts about Fay. Publications in this series are written in English and Chinese.

Series IV: Administrative Papers

physdesc.extent: in 4 manuscript boxes and 1 oversized flat box

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series documents the administrative and professional aspects of Ming Fay's career, including his organizational affiliations. Materials include correspondence, grant applications, artist residency records, resumes, biographies, artist statements, photographs of Fay's studio, address books, loan agreements, and budgets. The series also contains records relating to the destruction of several of Fay's sculptures in a fire at the Hunt's Point Sculpture Park in the Bronx.
